title: Point-gap topology of damped magnon excitations in skyrmion strings
abstract: We theoretically study the non-Hermitian topology of magnons with finite lifetimes due to Gilbert damping. By incorporating the spin-wave theory and perturbation theory for the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation including nonlocal damping terms, we analytically evaluate the spectral winding number for point gaps, which indicates the existence of the non-Hermitian skin effect (NHSE). We find that the NHSE can occur even in the absence of nonlocal damping. In the presence of nonlocal damping along one direction, we show that the winding number for an energy band with a unique minimum is determined from the sign of the wave number at the band minimum. We demonstrate these results using a model that hosts a skyrmion-string lattice as a steady state. We further investigate spin-wave propagation dynamics excited by a magnetic-field pulse and show that the propagation direction changes drastically from band to band depending on the presence of local and nonlocal damping, consistent with the nontrivial winding numbers.
summary: This paper explores how different types of magnetic damping lead to non-Hermitian topological features in magnon excitations within skyrmion strings. It demonstrates that both local and nonlocal damping can trigger the Non-Hermitian Skin Effect, causing spin waves to localize at specific boundaries or propagate in preferred directions. This work connects classical dissipative magnetism with modern non-Hermitian topological physics.
main_problem: Investigating the origin of non-Hermitian point-gap topology and the Non-Hermitian Skin Effect (NHSE) in the magnon spectra of skyrmion strings under local and nonlocal Gilbert damping.
main_result: The study finds that NHSE can occur even without nonlocal damping due to the momentum dependence of particle/hole weights, and that nonlocal damping can induce specific winding numbers that dictate the direction of spin-wave propagation.
method: Linear spin-wave theory and non-degenerate perturbation theory applied to the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation, supplemented by numerical simulations of skyrmion-string lattices.
model_or_system: A classical spin model hosting a skyrmion-string lattice as a steady state, described by a non-Hermitian Bogoliubov-de Gennes Hamiltonian derived from the LLG equation with local and nonlocal damping terms.
key_observables: Spectral winding number, complex energy spectrum, spin-wave propagation dynamics, and magnon localization at boundaries.
important_parameters: Gilbert damping coefficients (local alpha_0 and nonlocal alpha_parallel), magnetic field strength, and wave number at the band minimum.
main_assumptions: Zero temperature, classical spin vectors, small damping limit, and the assumption of a unique global band minimum for the real part of the energy.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the skyrmion-string lattice configuration; Fig 2 shows magnon band structures; Fig 5 and 6 demonstrate spin-wave propagation dynamics and asymmetric dissipation under different damping and polarization conditions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of non-Hermitian topology in magnons, develops an analytical framework using perturbation theory for the LLG equation, applies this to a skyrmion-string lattice model, and validates the results through numerical simulations of spin-wave dynamics.
why_interesting: The study provides deep insights into how dissipation (non-Hermitian physics) can induce topological phenomena like the skin effect and asymmetric transport, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and non-equilibrium many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Magnon band structures without damping along the momentum k = (0, 0, kz) in the Brillouin zone. The magnetic field is (a) B/b = 0.7 and (b) B/b = 0.2. The color indicates ηnkz defined by Eq. (18) for each eigenstate.

FIG. 1. Spin configuration of the stationary skyrmion-string- lattice state at B/b = 0.7 with b := JS/ℏ|γ|. We fix D/J = D∥/J∥= 1 and J∥/J = 1/2. Each arrow at site r represents a direction of the magnetic moment γSr of electrons (γ < 0). Green lines indicate the center of the skyrmions. The magnetic unit cell is shown as a gray parallelepiped.

FIG. 3. Magnon complex spectra at B/b = 0.7 with lo- cal damping only (α0 = 0.1, α∥= 0), (a) PBC; (b) OBC along the z direction with Nz = 51 sites along z. In (a), colors denote the momentum kz. In (b), colors denote ⟨z⟩:= ⟨ϕR n|z|ϕR n⟩/ ⟨ϕR n|ϕR n⟩, where |ϕR n⟩is the right eigenstate.

FIG. 4. Magnon complex spectra at B/b = 0.2 with nonlocal damping (α0 = 0.1, α∥= 0.05). (a) and (b) correspond to the PBC and the OBC along z (Nz = 51 sites), respectively. Color coding follows that in Fig. 3.

FIG. 5. Spin-wave propagation excited by a left-circularly rotating magnetic-field pulse B+ osc,r with frequency Ω= 1.5J/ℏ, applied only at the the single layer at z = 50c under the PBC in all directions for a system with Nz = 101 sites along z at B/b = 0.7. The color indicates the magnon number, normalized at every time t (a), (b), (d), (e). The pulse parameters are Bosc/b = 0.01, t0 = 5ℏ/J, σ = 6ℏ/J. The Gilbert damping is (a)–(c) α0 = α∥= 0 and (d)–(f) α0 = 0.1 and α∥= 0. (a), (d) Time evolution of the magnon number along the z axis. (b), (e) Corresponding time evolution in momentum space.
